1:
The great prostitute sitting on many waters is introduced as the anti-type of the Bride (nymphē, Revelation 21:2, 9). Where the Bride is clothed in Christ's righteousness (Revelation 19:8), the prostitute is clothed in imperial wealth and carries a cup of abominations. The structural parallelism forces a comparison: every human civilization offers itself as a bride to its inhabitants; only one Bride is legitimate.

The angel who shows John the prostitute (Revelation 17:1) uses the same verb (deiknymi) as the angel who shows him the Bride (Revelation 21:9) — the deliberate mirroring signals that the two visions interpret each other.
2:
The kings of the earth who committed fornication with Babylon (Revelation 17:2) are those who sought security, legitimacy, and economic benefit from the imperial cult system rather than from the Lamb. The contrast with 17:14 is stark: the kings who align with the beast go to war against the Lamb; the "called, chosen, and faithful" who are with the Lamb receive the opposite of what Babylon's clients receive.

The "wine of her immorality" echoes the prophetic condemnation of Babylon in Jeremiah 51:7 — Babylon made the nations drink and go mad. Christ by contrast offers the wine of the new covenant (Mark 14:24; John 6:53-56) that does not intoxicate but gives life.
3: The beast with seven heads and ten horns that the woman rides is the same beast of Revelation 13:1 — the political power that derives its authority from the dragon (Revelation 13:2), itself a parody of Christ who receives authority from the Father (Matthew 28:18; John 5:27). The color scarlet (kokkinos) echoes the dragon's red color (Revelation 12:3) and contrasts with the white linen of the Bride (Revelation 19:8). The woman rides but does not control the beast — as 17:16 will show, the beast ultimately turns on her.
4:
Purple and scarlet clothing, gold, jewels, and pearls (Revelation 17:4) are the same luxury goods mourned in Revelation 18:12-16, and they deliberately contrast with the Bride's linen "bright and pure" (Revelation 19:8). Where Babylon arrays herself in purchased wealth, the Bride is clothed in "righteous acts of the saints" — the gift of Christ's righteousness worked out in faithful suffering.

The golden cup full of abominations (bdelygmatōn) echoes Jesus's language for the desecrating sacrilege of Daniel 9:27 (Matthew 24:15) — Babylon's cup is a cup of judgment, the anti-type of the cup of the new covenant.
5:
"Mystery, Babylon the Great" written on her forehead mirrors the practice of Roman prostitutes who wore a placard with their name — the irony is that what Rome considers glory, God names as prostitution. The term "mystery" (mystērion) signals that this Babylon requires interpretation: it is not the historical Babylon destroyed in 539 BCE but the cipher for Rome, as Second Temple Jewish texts clearly show (4 Ezra 3:1-2; 1 Peter 5:13).

She is "the mother of prostitutes and of earth's abominations" — every city, empire, and power that demands ultimate loyalty from human beings participates in her spirit. The Christ-claim runs the opposite direction: "No one can serve two masters" (Matthew 6:24).
6:
"Drunk with the blood of the saints and the blood of the witnesses of Jesus" (Revelation 17:6) answers the martyrs' cry from beneath the altar in Revelation 6:9-11: "How long before you judge?" Babylon's guilt is precisely her murder of those whose testimony was Jesus. The phrase "witnesses of Jesus" (martyrōn Iēsou) connects directly to Revelation 1:2, 9; Revelation 12:17; Revelation 19:10 — to testify to Jesus is to share in the trajectory of the Lamb who was slain, and Babylon responds to that testimony with slaughter.

John's astonishment (ethaumasen) at the sight mirrors the astonishment of the earth-dwellers at the beast (Revelation 13:3) — the reader is meant to feel the seductiveness of imperial power before the interpretation that follows strips away the glamour.
7: The angel's explanatory discourse (Revelation 17:7-18) functions as apocalyptic interpretation of the vision — the same structure as Daniel 7-8 (vision followed by angelic interpretation). Christ is the key to reading apocalyptic: without the revelation given to the Lamb (Revelation 5:5-7), the sealed scroll of history remains closed. The angel who interprets is acting under the Lamb's authority.
8: The beast "that was, and is not, and is to come" (Revelation 17:8) is a direct parody of the divine formula "who is, and who was, and who is to come" (Revelation 1:4, 8; Revelation 4:8). The substitution of "is to come" (erchomenos) with "goes to destruction" (hypagei eis apōleian) reveals the beast's ultimate trajectory. Christ comes to reign; the beast comes only to be destroyed. The "wonder" of the earth-dwellers whose names are not in the book of life contrasts with the worship of those whose names are written there (Revelation 13:8; Revelation 21:27).
9: The seven hills identify Rome for any first-century reader. But the seven kings (Revelation 17:10-11) reveal that the vision is less about topography than about the succession of imperial power and its ultimate subordination to the Lamb's sovereignty. All human empires — however many there are — operate within a numbered sequence under divine permission.
10: The fallen five kings and the "one who is" situate the vision in historical time while maintaining the seven-as-completeness symbolism characteristic of Revelation. The "short time" of the sixth king parallels the "short time" the devil knows he has (Revelation 12:12) — a compressed urgency that frames the entire section. The Lamb has opened the scroll (Revelation 5:5-7); the sequence of powers unfolds within his sovereign timing.
11: The beast that "was and is not, it is an eighth and belongs to the seven" (Revelation 17:11) uses the symbolism of the ogdoad (eighth = beyond the week = new creation) but inverts it: the beast mimics resurrection and new-creation numbering. Christ is the genuine eighth — the first day of new creation, the resurrection day that stands beyond the Sabbath (John 20:1; 1 Corinthians 15:20). The beast's "eighth" is parody; Christ's is the substance.
12: The ten horns with authority "for one hour with the beast" are Parthian kings or, more broadly, all vassal powers who derive their authority from the beast-system. Their "one hour" authority contrasts with the Lamb's eternal kingdom (Revelation 11:15; Revelation 22:5). The pattern of delegated but temporary authority under the beast maps against the pattern of delegated but eternal authority under Christ (Daniel 7:13-14; Matthew 28:18; 1 Corinthians 15:27-28).
13: The unanimous giving of power and authority to the beast (Revelation 17:13) enacts the totalitarian claim of imperial power: it requires undivided loyalty. This is precisely the demand that the martyrs refused — they would not give their exousia to Caesar, which was what the imperial cult demanded in the oath of allegiance. The Lamb demands the same undivided loyalty (Matthew 10:37-38; Luke 9:23) but offers it as gift rather than coercion.
14: "They will make war on the Lamb, and the Lamb will conquer them" (Revelation 17:14) is the pivot of the entire Babylon vision. The mechanism of conquest is not military force but identity: he is "Lord of lords and King of kings" (the title that appears again at Revelation 19:16). Those with him are "called, chosen, and faithful" (klētoi kai eklektoi kai pistoi) — election language from the Synoptics (Matthew 22:14; cf. John 6:37-39; Romans 8:28-30) combined with the faithfulness that is the mark of the Lamb himself (Revelation 1:5; Revelation 3:14; Revelation 19:11).
15: "The waters where the prostitute sits are peoples and multitudes and nations and languages" (Revelation 17:15) — the same fourfold formula used of those who worship the beast (Revelation 13:7) and of those redeemed by the Lamb (Revelation 5:9; Revelation 7:9). The prostitute and the Lamb both have universal reach; the difference is that the Lamb purchases by his blood while the prostitute purchases by offering economic security and political legitimacy.
16: The beast and the ten kings turning to devour the prostitute (Revelation 17:16) shows that earthly political power is not a stable alliance. Babylon uses the beast but is ultimately consumed by it — "God has put it in their hearts to carry out his purpose" (Revelation 17:17). This is the theological claim that is axiomatic throughout Revelation: even the enemies of God serve his purposes. The Lamb who opens the seals (Revelation 6:1) remains sovereign even when evil turns on evil.
17: "For God has put it into their hearts to carry out his purpose" (Revelation 17:17) — the Christological content here is that the Father's purpose centers on the Son's vindication. The whole sweep of imperial history, including the destruction of the whore by the beast, is moving toward "the words of God are fulfilled" — a phrase that elsewhere in Revelation specifically refers to the disclosure of what the Lamb has opened (Revelation 10:7; Revelation 11:15).
18: The identification of the woman as "the great city that has dominion over the kings of the earth" (Revelation 17:18) closes the interpretation. For John's readers this was transparently Rome. But the figure of Babylon-as-great-city recurs throughout prophetic literature (Isa 47; Jer 50-51; Ezek 26-28) and points to every human city that organizes itself around self-sufficient power rather than God's reign. The New Jerusalem (Revelation 21:1-22:5) is the anti-type: the city whose builder and maker is God (Hebrews 11:10), whose light is the Lamb (Revelation 21:23).
